title: Engine 181 Car Fire Attack Line
scene: Engine 181 is positioned on the street for a working car fire. A single 1¾″ attack line is stretched from the engine to the vehicle. The picture has no built-in labels. All scenario labels are generated from this JSON using overlay coordinates.
studentQuestion: Calculate the pump pressure for the car fire attack line setup.
details: Engine 181 operating on tank water or hydrant supply
Single 1¾″ attack line stretched to a vehicle fire
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss
FL = C × (GPM ÷ 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds
instructorExplanation: For this car fire, calculate friction loss in the single 1¾″ attack line, then add the fog nozzle pressure. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss, so the pump pressure is friction loss plus nozzle pressure.
explainMistake: Common mistakes are using the wrong C value for 1¾″ hose, forgetting to convert 150′ into 1.5 hundred-foot sections, using 100 psi nozzle pressure when the question says 50 psi, adding appliance loss when there is no appliance, or rounding friction loss too early.

variations.change: Base question: 150′ of 1¾″ hose with a fog nozzle flowing 150 gpm at 50 psi.
variations.question: Engine 181 is operating at a car fire. The crew stretched 150′ of 1¾″ hose with a fog nozzle flowing 150 gpm at 50 psi. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
details: Engine 181 operating at a vehicle fire
formulaBreakdown: PP = 52 FL + 50 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
variations.instructorExplanation: Use the 1¾″ hose friction loss for the 150′ stretch, then add 50 psi nozzle pressure.

variations.change: Increase the attack line length to 200′. Keep the fog nozzle at 150 gpm and 50 psi.
variations.question: Same picture, new setup: Engine 181 is operating at a car fire. The crew stretched 200′ of 1¾″ hose with a fog nozzle flowing 150 gpm at 50 psi. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
details: Engine 181 operating at a vehicle fire
formulaBreakdown: PP = 70 FL + 50 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
variations.instructorExplanation: Only the hose length changed. The longer 1¾″ stretch increases friction loss from 52 psi to 70 psi.

variations.change: Increase the fog nozzle flow to 185 gpm. Keep the line length at 150′ and nozzle pressure at 50 psi.
variations.question: Same picture, new setup: Engine 181 is operating at a car fire. The crew stretched 150′ of 1¾″ hose with a fog nozzle flowing 185 gpm at 50 psi. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
details: Engine 181 operating at a vehicle fire
formulaBreakdown: PP = 80 FL + 50 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
variations.instructorExplanation: Only the flow changed. The higher fog nozzle flow increases friction loss in the 1¾″ attack line.

variations.change: Change the nozzle package to a 100 psi fog nozzle. Keep 150′ of 1¾″ hose flowing 150 gpm.
variations.question: Same picture, new setup: Engine 181 is operating at a car fire. The crew stretched 150′ of 1¾″ hose with a fog nozzle flowing 150 gpm at 100 psi. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
details: Engine 181 operating at a vehicle fire
formulaBreakdown: PP = 52 FL + 100 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
variations.instructorExplanation: Only the nozzle pressure changed. The hose friction loss stays 52 psi, but the nozzle pressure increases to 100 psi.
